text: The surgeon docks on the right-hand side and begins dissection in the left lower retro-rectus space, keeping the epigastric vessels against the rectus muscle. The posterior sheath is separated from the edge of the left side of the hernia defect. A gap in the posterior sheath is observed, possibly indicating semilunar line attenuation. The surgeon removes mesh from the rectus muscle to preserve posterior closure, navigating through scarred tissue and finding a free space for easier dissection. The peritoneum bowing into the abdominal cavity is taken down to aid in posterior closure.

The surgeon observes a full disruption in the middle of the Progrit mesh, with a small intact bridge. The surgeon excises this portion to create a common hernia defect. They attempt to stay on the medial edge of the rectus muscle, navigating through the hernia sac and scar tissue to re-enter the retro-rectus space. The surgeon carefully removes the mesh from the rectus muscle, avoiding damage, and proceeds to develop the space of Retzius in the hypogastrium, maintaining the epigastric vessels against the rectus muscle.

The surgeon identifies the arcuate line on the left side and begins a 'bottoms up' transversus abdominis release (TAR) to define the anatomy near the epigastrium. The dissection progresses laterally before returning medially, ensuring the correct surgical plane is maintained.

The surgeon navigates a scarred surgical field, identifying a neurovascular bundle within the plane. They carefully dissect while staying against the peritoneum and transversus abdominis muscle, avoiding damage to the semilunar line.

The surgeon begins dissecting from the superior edge of the hernia defect, attempting to re-enter the proper surgical plane. As they navigate through the scar tissue, muscle movement is observed, indicating potential interaction with the transversus abdominis muscle. The surgeon adjusts their approach, moving slightly medial within the hernia defect to better identify the transversus abdominis, while maintaining proximity to the back of the rectus muscle.

The surgeon activates a surgical tool to assist in the dissection of the scarred plane between the rectus muscle and the mesh. The peritoneum is being carefully separated from the rectus muscle, revealing the lateral edge of the mesh. The surgeon navigates the semilunar line and connects the dissection plane from the inferior tar to the current area.

The surgeon continues the transversus abdominis release, dissecting through the scar plate near the semilunar line. The area shows signs of scarring from a previous surgery, where the peritoneal flap and posterior sheath flap were sutured closed.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the back of the rectus muscle, navigating through scar tissue on the lateral portion near the mesh. The transversus abdominis muscle is identified, and the surgeon maintains the dissection plane between the muscle and the peritoneum. The area appears less scarred as the dissection progresses laterally, allowing for easier closure of defects. The transversus abdominis muscle is observed in the middle of a posterior gap, with no visible defect in the semilunar line.

The surgeon continues to work laterally and then returns medially, developing space above and below the surgical area. The dissection is widened and elevated as much as possible above the costal margin, focusing on the superior edge of the defect. The xiphoid process is visible, and the surgeon dissects subxiphoid, connecting the previous dissection from the right to the left side. The diaphragm's movement is visible at the top of the screen.

The surgeon continues the dissection, carefully navigating near the diaphragm and costal margin to extend the dissection inferiorly for optimal mesh overlap. The peritoneum is gently pulled down, revealing the full length of the dissection, which is completed successfully.
